title: Extension Points
use_for: defining plugin or module capability contracts
keeping extensibility reviewable without service locator drift
separating host-owned lifecycle from feature-provided capabilities
making extension boundaries narrow and stable
do_not_use_for: raw bootstrap order without extension concerns
rules: define extension contracts around capabilities, not around internal host objects
keep extension surfaces narrow and versionable
host owns lifecycle while extensions supply capability
prefer explicit registration over hidden discovery
procedure: Identify the host capability that should be extendable.
Define registration and lifecycle ownership boundaries.
Specify failure, ordering, and compatibility rules.
Verify the extension surface does not leak host internals.

primary_when: the request is specifically about plugin contracts, host extensibility, module hooks, or extension-surface design
the request is about exposing capability extension without leaking host internals
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about one concrete feature implementation, UI composition, or startup sequencing with no extension surface

solves: how to let features or modules extend a host without hidden coupling
how to keep extension APIs capability-focused and reviewable
how to separate host lifecycle ownership from extension-provided behavior
does_not_solve: one-off internal abstractions with no extension boundary

core_principles: An extension point should publish capability, not host internals.
The host keeps lifecycle authority even when behavior is extensible.
Extensions should register explicitly so review can see what enters the system.
constraints: Extension boundaries must stay narrow enough to version and review.
Registration should not become hidden discovery or reflection-driven orchestration.
Failure in one extension should have an explicit containment or reporting path.
decision_rules: Use extension-points as the owner when the main design question is how a host exposes extensibility safely.
If extensions participate in runtime phases, attach runtime-lifecycle to keep host ownership explicit.
If extensions require registration and construction policy, attach DI or bootstrap only after the contract surface is stable.
If an extension contract mirrors host internals, redesign the boundary around the capability actually needed.

preferred_patterns: explicit registration at known host boundaries
host-owned lifecycle with extension callbacks constrained by contract
diagnostic visibility around extension load and failure
avoid_patterns: broad plugin interfaces exposing host service bags
reflection-driven extension discovery as the default path
extensions mutating host internals through hidden backchannels
unbounded hook surfaces with unclear compatibility rules

recipes.name: Design a narrow host extension API
recipes.when: Use when a runtime host must allow modules or plugins to add behavior without leaking host internals.
steps: Name the host capability being extended.
Define the minimal extension contract and registration point.
Specify which lifecycle phases the extension may participate in.
Define ordering and failure reporting rules explicitly.

pitfalls: Turning extensibility into a disguised service locator surface.
Letting extension contracts expose concrete host types and internals.
Leaving extension ordering or failure behavior implicit.
review_checklist: Does the host retain lifecycle authority?
Are failure and ordering rules visible in the design?

references.title: Local Reference: Extension Points
references.interpretation: Use this first for host extensibility, plugin contract shape, and registration-boundary policy.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for explicit extensibility design.

references.title: Local Reference: Framework Contracts
references.interpretation: Use this to keep extension surfaces narrow, capability-oriented, and compatible with the broader contract model.
references.why: Use to align extensibility design with the framework's boundary discipline.
